# 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--04--15_09:58:26-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 98 English News Articles published between 2026-04-08 and 2026-04-14.*

## 1. Sri Lanka and the IMF reached a staff-level agreement on the fifth and sixth reviews of the four-year Extended Fund Facility

The deal unlocked roughly $700m 💰, lifting cumulative program disbursements to about $2.4bn, pending Executive Board approval. Authorities recorded low-single-digit inflation, stronger revenue, rebuilt reserves, and continued debt-restructuring progress, while being urged to keep fiscal discipline and accelerate structural reforms to cement a fragile recovery.

## 2. Colombo Stock Exchange rallied after a two-week ceasefire was announced in the Middle East

The bourse surged 4.21% to 21,917.60 on its broad gauge, while the blue-chip index climbed 4.42% to 6,090.50, with turnover at Rs. 6.61 billion and 11 crossings recorded. Gains were led by capital goods, banks and diversified groups, and the rupee strengthened modestly in spot trading as bond yields eased.

## 3. A day later, local equities turned volatile after large-scale air strikes in Lebanon

The market slipped by 73.06 points on the all-share benchmark as turnover slowed to Rs. 2.96 billion, even as the large-cap index eked out a modest rise with help from selective crossings. Traders also weighed the presence of an international lender’s review team, which supported sentiment despite elevated headline risk.

## 4. Parliament scrutinised a Rs. 13.2 billion fraud reported at National Development Bank

Lawmakers pressed regulators on why initially disclosed losses mushroomed within days, while supervisors imposed a dividend freeze, curtailed discretionary spending and branch expansion, and required an independent forensic audit. Ministers confirmed the head of state had been briefed and pledged measures to safeguard sector discipline as committee hearings gathered pace.

## 5. Government launched a Rs. 2.16 billion modernisation of Colombo Fort Railway Station with 15‑month completion target

Planned works include automated ticketing, lifts and overhead bridges for accessibility, upgraded sanitation, and new passenger-safety facilities under an ADB-supported efficiency project. The ceremony, attended by cabinet and parliamentary leaders, set out a shift to passenger‑centric design to serve hundreds of thousands of daily commuters.

## 6. Train services on the Northern Line resumed after India-funded repairs to cyclone-damaged sections

Restoration was financed via a $5 million grant drawn from a broader $450 million package and completed ahead of the Sinhala and Tamil New Year. The reopening reestablished connectivity across impacted districts, easing passenger flows and freight movement after disruption from Cyclone Ditwah.

## 7. Cabinet approved the appointment of H. M. J. M. Herath as Commissioner General of Registration of Persons

The senior administrative officer, serving as Kegalle’s Government Agent, was tapped to fill a vacancy that had been covered in an acting capacity by a ministry official. The move aimed to accelerate rollout of the digital identity card and stabilise day‑to‑day operations; the nomination was tabled by the minister responsible for the digital economy.

## 8. Parliamentary Select Committee examined legal options to end the Provincial Council election impasse

Officials told members that incomplete delimitation under the 2017 mixed system left no operative framework to conduct polls, noting a review report was never finalised. Alternatives under study include repealing the amendment to reinstate the prior law, incorporating stronger provisions for women’s and youth representation, and seeking Attorney General guidance on Supreme Court rulings before summoning the Delimitation Commission.

## 9. Energy transition gathered pace as Hayleys completed an 800 kW rooftop solar project for LSEG Sri Lanka

The six‑building installation of 1,412 panels is expected to generate roughly 1,250 MWh annually, meeting close to 80% of daytime demand and avoiding about 850 tonnes of CO₂ per year ⚡. Engineers delivered the project within two months with zero accidents, including complex underground cabling across an active corporate site.

## 10. Commercial Bank and NCG Express rolled out fully digital bus ticketing across routes in a large private operator’s fleet

Conductors began using handheld devices to accept card taps, mobile wallets and QR scans, with instant settlement through an acquiring network and printed confirmations. The rollout, aligned with the national digital ticketing initiative, reduced cash handling, improved auditability and offered passengers faster, exact‑fare payments, with initial deployments live and a phased expansion planned.

## Model Prompt (for [gpt-5](https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5))

```
You will be given several Sri Lankan news articles from the past week. Write a concise, cohesive editorial digest in the style of *The Economist*, output as a JSON list. Each list element must be a dict of the form:

```json
{
  "title": "...",
  "body": "..."
}
```

**Length:** The complete digest should contain exactly 10 items. Each item should have a body of 2–3 sentences, resulting in a total of roughly 800–1000 words across all body paragraphs combined.

**Titles and body as a single continuous narrative:** Write each item so that the title is the opening clause and the body continues directly from it — together they form one unbroken piece of prose. No fact, figure, or proper noun introduced in the title should be repeated in the body. The body picks up where the title leaves off, adding new information only.

*Example of the intended style:*
> **Title:** Sri Lanka and the IMF reached a staff-level agreement on the fifth and sixth reviews of the four-year Extended Fund Facility
> **Body:** The deal unlocked roughly $700m, lifting total disbursements under the programme to approximately $2.4bn 💰, subject to Executive Board approval.

**Tense:**
- Use the **past tense** for all events that have already occurred.
- Use the **future tense** only for events explicitly scheduled or confirmed to occur after the date of the source articles.
- Do not use the present tense to describe past events (e.g. write "the government passed" not "the government passes" or "the government has passed").

**Titles** must read like headlines from a reputable newspaper (think *Financial Times* or *The Economist*) — precise, active, and newsy — but when read in sequence, they should form a narrative arc that shows how the week's events connect and build on one another. Titles must contain only verifiable facts, no opinion or editorialising.

**Body** paragraphs must add context and nuance beyond the headline. Do not use bullet points. Explain any non-obvious acronyms or jargon on first use. Use emojis purposefully and sparingly where they aid scannability (e.g. 💰 for fiscal matters, ⚡ for energy).

**Strict factual standard — apply to every sentence:**

- Include only **events that occurred** (votes taken, laws passed, figures released, agreements signed, arrests made, prices recorded) and **objective facts** (statistics, measurements, named parties to a transaction, official outcomes).
- **Exclude entirely:** statements, quotes, promises, warnings, predictions, denials, calls for action, expressions of concern, and any other speech acts — even from officials or institutions. The fact that someone *said* something is not itself a fact about the world.
- If the only available information on a topic is what someone claimed or announced, omit that topic rather than report the claim as fact.
- Do not speculate, editorialize, or characterise intent or motivation.

**Causality standard — apply to every title and sentence:**

- Report events as separate, independent facts. Do not imply that one event caused another unless a direct causal mechanism is part of the established, measurable record.
- Avoid constructions such as "as", "following", "amid", "boosting", "driven by", or "in response to" that suggest one event caused or coincided meaningfully with another. Multiple factors almost always contribute to any outcome, and implying a single cause is a form of editorialising.
- If two events occurred in the same week, report them separately. Do not place them in the same sentence or title in a way that implies a relationship between them.

The digest as a whole should read as a coherent account of the week, not a list of unrelated summaries.
```
